transcript: Welcome back. In the last lesson, you learned how to read and interpret campaign reports inside GoHighLevel using the CBAR framework. You now know how to analyze email performance, workflow completion rates, and pipeline stage conversions. But there is a significant gap in what GoHighLevel can show you. And that gap is everything that happens before someone fills out a form. In this lesson, we are going to close that gap using Google Analytics 4 and UTM parameters. Let's get into it. Here's a question I want you to think about. You build a landing page. You set up a workflow. Leads start coming in. Go high level shows you the form submissions. The email opens. the pipeline movements, all of that is tracked. But here is what GHL cannot tell you. Where did those leads actually come from? Were they from a Facebook ad? An email broadcast you sent? An organic Google search? A link you posted on Instagram? Without that information, you are making decisions blindly. You might be spending a thousand pesos a day on Facebook ads thinking they are driving leads, when in reality 80% of your leads are coming from an old blog post. Or the reverse, your ads are working brilliantly, but you have no proof to show the client. Google Analytics 4 fills that gap. GHL tracks what happens inside the platform after the lead arrives. GA4 tracks what happens before that, where the traffic came from, how many people visited the page, how long they stayed, and whether they converted. Used together, these two tools give you complete attribution from the very first click to the final sale. Before we get into UTM parameters, let me orient you to the four sections of Google Analytics 4 that you will use most as a marketing automation specialist. The first is reports overview. This is the home screen of GA4 and it gives you a high-level snapshot of total users, active sessions, and conversion events for whatever date range you select. Think of it as your morning check-in, a quick look at the overall health of your traffic. The second is acquisition reports. This is the most important section for our purposes, and we will spend the most time here. Acquisition reports show you exactly where your traffic came from, whether that is organic search, paid ads, direct visits, email campaigns, social media, or any other source you have tagged with UTM parameters. The third is engagement reports. This section shows you what users actually did on the site after they arrived. Which pages did they view? How long did they stay? Did they scroll to the bottom of the landing page or bounce immediately? This data tells you whether your content is holding attention. The fourth is conversions. This section shows you how many users completed a defined goal event, such as reaching a thank you page after submitting a form, clicking a call booking button, or purchasing a product. Conversion tracking in GA4 requires some setup, which we will cover shortly. The simple way to remember it, acquisition answers where they came from. Engagement answers what they did. Conversions answers whether they took the action you wanted Now let us talk about UTM parameters because this is the foundational skill that makes everything else in Google Analytics meaningful UTM stands for Urchin Tracking Module, but the name does not matter. What matters is what UTM parameters do. They are labels you attach to the end of any URL, so that Google Analytics can identify exactly where a click came from. There are five UTM parameters, and each one answers a different question. The first is UTM underscore source. This answers, where is the traffic coming from? Examples include Facebook, Google, Instagram, and newsletter. This is a required parameter. The second is UTM underscore medium. This answers, what type of marketing channel sent the traffic? Examples include social, email, CPC for paid search, SMS, and organic. This is also required. The third is UTM underscore campaign. This answers, which specific campaign is this link part of? Examples include free-guide-launch, january-promo, and discovery-call-offer. This parameter is not required, but you should always include it because it makes your reports far more readable. The fourth is UTM underscore content. This answers, which specific ad, button, or link variation sent the traffic. This is used when you are running multiple ads in the same campaign and want to know which one performed better. Examples include Hero Image Button, Sidebar Link, and Email CTA2. The fifth is UTM underscore term. This answers, what keyword triggered a paid search ad? This is used exclusively for paid search campaigns and is the parameter you will use least often as a marketing automation specialist. For most of your work, you will use source, medium, and campaign on every link and add content when you are running A-B tests on ads or email CTS. Let me show you what a complete UTM tagged URL looks like in practice. Start with your base URL. For example, yoursite.com slash free-guide. Then add a question mark to begin the parameters. Then add each parameter connected by an ampersand symbol. The complete URL looks like this. Translated into plain language, that URL tells Google Analytics. A visitor arrived at the free guide landing page from a Facebook carousel ad as part of the free guide launch campaign. Every parameter you add gives you one more layer of attribution clarity. And the best part is that the visitor sees none of this. The UTM parameters are invisible to them in their browser and do not affect the page they land on in any way. Two important rules for UTM formatting. First always use lowercase GA4 is case sensitive and Facebook and Facebook will appear as two separate traffic sources in your reports splitting your data and creating confusion Second use hyphens instead of spaces. Spaces in URLs create broken links or get encoded into percent signs, both of which damage you're tracking. You should never build UTM URLs by hand. Google provides a free tool called the Campaign URL Builder at ga-dev-tools.google.com slash campaign-url-builder. You simply fill in the five fields. Website URL, Campaign Source, Campaign Medium, campaign name, campaign content, and campaign term. And the tool generates the complete tagged URL for you automatically. From there, you copy the generated URL and use it in your Facebook ad, your email call to action button, your SMS message link, or wherever you want to track that specific click. Inside your template, you will find a pre-built naming convention guide that makes this process consistent across every client you work with. Now let us look at how to read what GA4 shows you. Once your UTM links are live and collecting data. Inside the traffic acquisition report, you will see a table with columns for session source and medium, users, sessions, engaged sessions, engagement rate, and conversions. Each row represents one traffic source. Let me walk you through a real example with three traffic sources. Facebook Social brought in 1,420 users across 1,680 sessions, with 840 engaged sessions, a 50% engagement rate, and 68 conversions. Google CPC brought in 890 users across 1,100 sessions, with 710 engaged sessions, a 64.5% engagement rate, and 54 conversions. Email newsletter brought in 540 users across 580 sessions, with 490 engaged sessions, an 84.5% engagement rate, and 62 conversions. Here is the insight that raw session counts would completely hide. Email traffic brought in the fewest users but had the highest engagement rate and nearly matched paid search in total conversions. That means email subscribers are the highest quality traffic source in this example. And if you were only looking at session volume, you would completely miss that finding. This is exactly why the CVAR framework from Lesson 8.3 applies here too. Context What was the goal? Benchmark How does engagement rate compare across sources? Anomaly Why is email engagement so high despite low volume? Recommendation Invest in growing the email list and increase email broadcast frequency. Inside Google Analytics 4, open the left sidebar and navigate to Reports, then Acquisition, then Traffic Acquisition. The primary dimension will default to Session Default Channel Group, which groups traffic into broad categories. To see your UTM tagged data, click the Dimension Selector dropdown at the top of the table and change it to Session Source and Medium. This will show every unique combination of source and medium that has sent traffic to your site including all of your UTM campaigns Use the date range selector in the top right to match the period you are analyzing Typically the same period covered by the GHL campaign report you completed in Lesson 8.3. Here is how GA4 and GHL fit together into a complete attribution picture. Think of it as a three-stage chain. In Stage 1, which happens in GA4, a visitor lands on your landing page via a UTM-tagged URL. GA4 records the source, medium, and campaign. The visitor fills out the form. GA4 records a conversion event. In Stage 2, which is the bridge between the two platforms, the UTM parameters are passed through the form submission into the contact record in GHL. This means the contact's record in GHL shows where they originally came from. When you set up your forms in GHL, make sure the hidden fields for UTM parameters are included. This is a setup step from Lesson 8.2 that connects the two platforms. In stage 3, which happens inside GHL, the workflow fires, the welcome email sends, the contact opens the email and clicks a link, GHL logs those engagement events, and tags and pipeline stages update based on behavior. The result is that GA4 tells you which traffic source produced the lead, and GHL tells you what that lead did after they arrived. When you combine those two datasets in your reporting, you can answer the question that every client wants answered. Which marketing channel is actually producing paying customers, not just leads? Let us close with six key takeaways from this lesson. First, Go High-Level tracks what happens after the form submission. Google Analytics 4 tracks what happens before it. You need both to have complete attribution. Second, the 4G. A4 sections you will use most are reports overview, acquisition, engagement, and conversions. Acquisition is the most important for source tracking. Third, the five UTM parameters are source, medium, campaign, content, and term. Source and medium are always required. Campaign should always be included. Content is used for A-B ad testing Term is used for paid search keywords only Fourth, always use lowercase letters and hyphens in your UTM parameters GA4 is case sensitive and inconsistent naming will split your data and corrupt your reports Fifth, use Google's free campaign URL builder to generate tagged URLs Never build them by hand. Sixth, when reading the traffic acquisition report, do not just look at session volume. Look at engagement rate and conversions per source. The highest volume traffic source is rarely the highest quality one. In the next lesson, lesson 8.5, we are going to take everything you have learned about tracking and reporting and apply it to one of the most powerful optimization tools available. a slash b testing. You will learn what to test, how to design a valid test, how to read the results, and how to implement the winning variation without breaking what is already working. I will see you there.

visualDescriptions.description:
The image presents a comparison between two data tools, GoHighLevel and Google Analytics 4, highlighting their respective areas of focus and the insights they provide.

* **Title**: 
	+ The title of the image is "GHL Tracks What Happens Inside. GA4 Tracks What Happens Before."
	+ It emphasizes the distinct functionalities of GoHighLevel (GHL) and Google Analytics 4 (GA4).
* **Subtitle**:
	+ The subtitle reads, "Understanding the visibility gap between your two most important data tools."
	+ It sets the context for understanding the complementary nature of GHL and GA4.
* **GoHighLevel sees: Post-conversion & automation actions**
	+ Form submissions
	+ Email opens & clicks
	+ Workflow completions
	+ Pipeline movements
	+ SMS delivery & replies
	+ Tag assignments
* **Google Analytics 4 sees: Pre-conversion traffic behavior**
	+ Where traffic came from (Source/Medium)
	+ How many people visited
	+ How long they stayed
	+ Pages viewed before converting
	+ Bounce rate & engagement
	+ Device and geographic data

The image effectively illustrates how GoHighLevel and Google Analytics 4 serve different purposes in tracking user interactions, with GoHighLevel focusing on post-conversion actions and automation, while Google Analytics 4 provides insights into pre-conversion traffic behavior. By combining these tools, users can gain a comprehensive understanding of their audience's journey. 

The green button at the bottom of the image reads, "Together, these two tools give you the complete picture." This emphasizes the value of using both tools in conjunction to achieve a fuller understanding of user behavior.

The image presents a comprehensive overview of the four essential sections in Google Analytics 4 (GA4) for understanding core dashboards related to traffic and performance analysis. The title, "The Four GA4 Sections You Need to Know," sets the stage for an informative visual guide.

**Reports Snapshot**
*   **Purpose:** The "Pulse Check"
    *   Is traffic generally trending up or down?
    *   Are there any sudden spikes or drops?
    *   Quick view of real-time active users.

**Acquisition Reports**
*   **Purpose:** Identify Top Channels
    *   User Acquisition: How new people find you.
    *   Traffic Acquisition: Where all sessions come from.
    *   Key Metric: Session Source / Medium.

**Engagement Reports**
*   **Purpose:** Content Performance
    *   Which pages keep people reading?
    *   Average engagement time per session.
    *   Event counts (clicks, scrolls, video plays).

**Conversions / Goals**
*   **Purpose:** Success Measurement
    *   Did they do what we wanted them to do?
    *   Conversion Rate per source.
    *   "Key Events" (formerly Goals).

The image provides a clear and concise breakdown of each section's purpose and key metrics, making it easier for users to understand and navigate GA4's core dashboards. A summary at the bottom of the image reinforces the main points: Acquisition (where they came from), Engagement (what they did), and Conversions (did they take the goal action?). Overall, the image effectively communicates the importance of these four sections in analyzing website traffic and performance.

The image presents a table illustrating the five UTM parameters used to track links, along with their definitions and examples. The title of the image is "UTM Parameters: The Five Labels That Tag Every Link."

**Table Structure**

The table consists of three columns:

*   **PARAMETER**: Lists the five UTM parameters.
*   **WHAT IT ANSWERS**: Provides a brief description of what each parameter answers.
*   **EXAMPLES**: Offers examples for each parameter.

**UTM Parameters and Descriptions**

The table outlines the following UTM parameters:

*   **utm_source**
    *   What it answers: Where is the traffic coming from?
    *   Examples: facebook, google, newsletter, instagram
*   **utm_medium**
    *   What it answers: What type of marketing channel sent them?
    *   Examples: social, email, cpc, sms
*   **utm_campaign**
    *   What it answers: Which specific campaign is this?
    *   Examples: free-guide-launch, jan-promo, discovery-call-offer
*   **utm_content**
    *   What it answers: Which specific ad or link variant sent them?
    *   Examples: hero-image-button, sidebar-link, email-cta-2
*   **utm_term**
    *   What it answers: What keyword triggered a paid search ad?
    *   Examples: marketing-automation-specialist, ghl-course

**Requirements and Recommendations**

At the bottom of the image, a note indicates that:

*   **REQUIRED**: utm_source and utm_medium
*   **STRONGLY RECOMMENDED**: utm_campaign
*   **OPTIONAL**: utm_content and utm_term are used for advanced split-tracking.

In summary, the image provides a clear and concise overview of the five UTM parameters, their purposes, and examples, as well as guidelines on which ones are required, recommended, or optional.

The image presents a comprehensive breakdown of what a UTM URL looks like, providing a clear and concise explanation of its components.

* **What a UTM URL Actually Looks Like**
	+ The title is prominently displayed at the top of the image.
* **UTM URL Breakdown**
	+ The UTM URL is presented in a dark blue box with a white background.
	+ The URL is: `yoursite.com/free-guide?utm_source=facebook&utm_medium=social&utm_campaign=free-guide-launch&utm_content=carousel-ad-1`
	+ The URL is broken down into its components:
		- **BASE URL**: `yoursite.com/free-guide`
		- **SOURCE**: `utm_source=facebook` (green)
		- **MEDIUM**: `utm_medium=social` (blue)
		- **CAMPAIGN**: `utm_campaign=free-guide-launch` (orange)
		- **CONTENT**: `utm_content=carousel-ad-1` (purple)
* **Plain-Language Translation**
	+ A plain-language translation of the UTM URL is provided below the URL breakdown.
	+ The translation reads: "A visitor arrived at the Free Guide landing page from a Facebook Social post, clicking a carousel ad as part of the Free Guide Launch campaign."
* **Additional Information**
	+ A note at the bottom of the image states: "Every parameter adds one more layer of attribution clarity."

In summary, the image provides a clear and concise explanation of what a UTM URL looks like, breaking it down into its individual components and providing a plain-language translation of its meaning. The image effectively communicates the importance of UTM URLs in tracking the source of website traffic and understanding the effectiveness of marketing campaigns.

The image presents a screenshot of the Google Campaign URL Builder tool, which is a free online resource provided by Google. The tool allows users to generate UTM links without manually typing them.

*   **Title and URL**
    *   The title "Google Campaign URL Builder" is displayed prominently at the top of the image.
    *   The URL for the tool is provided: ga-dev-tools.google.com/campaign-url-builder/
*   **Campaign URL Builder Form**
    *   The form consists of four input fields:
        *   **Base URL**: The base URL of the website, e.g., yoursite.com/free-guide
        *   **Source**: The source of the campaign, e.g., facebook
        *   **Medium**: The medium of the campaign, e.g., social
        *   **Campaign**: The campaign name, e.g., free-guide-launch
        *   **Content**: The content of the campaign, e.g., carousel-ad-1
    *   A blue button labeled "Copy URL" is provided to copy the generated URL.
*   **Generated URL**
    *   The generated URL is displayed below the form: yoursite.com/free-guide?utm_source=facebook&utm_medium=social&utm_campaign=free-guide-launch&utm_content=carousel-ad-1
*   **Step-by-Step Process**
    *   The image illustrates a step-by-step process to generate a UTM link:
        1.  Base URL
        2.  Source
        3.  Medium
        4.  Campaign
        5.  Generated Result

In summary, the Google Campaign URL Builder tool provides a user-friendly interface to generate UTM links by filling out a simple form with campaign details. The tool automatically generates a URL that can be used to track the performance of online campaigns.

The image presents a table titled "Reading the Traffic Acquisition Report in GA4" and features a logo for "Libre" in the top-right corner. The table is divided into six columns, each representing different metrics: Session Source/Medium, Users, Sessions, Engaged Sess., Engage Rate, and Conversions.

**Table Data:**

*   **facebook / social**
    *   Users: 1,420
    *   Sessions: 1,680
    *   Engaged Sess.: 840
    *   Engage Rate: 50.0%
    *   Conversions: 68
*   **google / cpc**
    *   Users: 890
    *   Sessions: 1,100
    *   Engaged Sess.: 710
    *   Engage Rate: 64.5%
    *   Conversions: 54
*   **email / newsletter**
    *   Users: 540
    *   Sessions: 580
    *   Engaged Sess.: 490
    *   Engage Rate: 84.5%
    *   Conversions: 62

**Strategic Insight:**

A black box at the bottom of the image provides a strategic insight, highlighting that email traffic has the highest engagement rate (84.5%) and nearly matches paid search in total conversions (62 vs 54) despite having far fewer sessions. A raw session count would hide this efficiency.

**Key Takeaways:**

*   The table provides a comparison of traffic acquisition metrics across three sources: Facebook, Google, and Email.
*   Email traffic exhibits the highest engagement rate and competitive conversion numbers despite lower session counts.
*   The strategic insight emphasizes the importance of considering engagement rates and conversion metrics beyond just session counts when evaluating traffic acquisition efficiency.

The image presents a comprehensive overview of the data handoff process between GA4 and GHL, providing insights into how these two tools work together to track and analyze user interactions. The image is divided into three main sections: GA4: Pre-Conversion, The Bridge: Data Handoff, and GHL: Post-Conversion.

**GA4: Pre-Conversion**

* **Visitor Lands**
	+ Arrives via UTM-tagged URL
* **Tracking Active**
	+ GA4 captures source, medium, & campaign
* **Form Submission**
	+ Visitor fills out the GHL form
* **Conversion Event**
	+ GA4 logs the goal completion

**The Bridge: Data Handoff**

* **Hidden Fields**
	+ UTM parameters pass silently through form
* **Contact Record**
	+ GHL creates contact & saves UTM data
* **Workflow Trigger**
	+ Contact enters the "New Lead" automation

**GHL: Post-Conversion**

* **Communication**
	+ Workflow fires Welcome Email/SMS
* **Engagement**
	+ Contact opens email or clicks link
* **Updates & Tags**
	+ Pipeline stage moves & tags update

**Key Takeaways**

* GA4 tells you which traffic source produced the lead
* GHL tells you what that lead did after they arrived
* Attribution is complete only when both tools are used together

In summary, the image provides a clear and concise visual representation of the data handoff process between GA4 and GHL, highlighting the key steps involved in tracking user interactions from pre-conversion to post-conversion. By understanding this process, businesses can gain valuable insights into their marketing efforts and make data-driven decisions to optimize their strategies.

The image presents a concise summary of key takeaways from a lesson, likely related to Google Analytics and marketing. The main points are:

• **Pre vs. Post Conversion**: 
	+ GA4 shows pre-conversion behavior and traffic sources.
	+ GHL shows post-conversion actions and outcomes.

• **UTM Essentials**: 
	+ UTM parameters standardize attribution.
	+ Source and medium are required.
	+ Campaign is strongly recommended.

• **Error-Free Links**: 
	+ Build clean UTM URLs using Google's URL Builder.
	+ Avoid typos and ensure accurate tracking.

• **The Big Three**: 
	+ In GA4, focus on Acquisition (source/medium), Engagement, and Conversions.

• **Quality Over Volume**: 
	+ Read the Traffic Acquisition report to compare channel quality (engagement/conversions), not just raw traffic volume.

• **Full Attribution**: 
	+ Connect GA4 and GHL data to see both where leads came from and what happened after they arrived.

The image provides a clear and concise overview of the key takeaways from the lesson, highlighting important concepts and best practices for using Google Analytics and UTM parameters effectively.
